title-group.article-title: Targeted Drugs, PADN, and Transcatheter Closure for Atrial Septal Defect–Associated Pulmonary Hypertension

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Cardiology, Zhongshan Hospital, Shanghai Institute of Cardiovascular Diseases, Fudan University, Shanghai, China
bNational Clinical Research Center for Interventional Medicine, Shanghai, China

author-notes.corresp: ∗Address for correspondence: Dr Wenzhi Pan, Zhongshan Hospital, Fudan University, 180 Fenglin Road, Xuhui District, Shanghai 200032, China. peden@sina.com

fn.p: These authors contributed equally to this work.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Targeted drug therapy and subsequent transcatheter closure are effective in treating atrial septal defects in patients with severe pulmonary arterial hypertension. Nevertheless, despite sufficient pharmacological therapy, many patients do not satisfy the hemodynamic criteria for transcatheter closure under the current guidelines. Here, we report 2 patients with atrial septal defect complicated by pulmonary arterial hypertension, in which successful transcatheter occlusion was achieved through prodromal targeted drug therapy combined with pulmonary artery denervation ablation (PADN). PADN can improve hemodynamic variables in patients who receive adequate targeted drug therapy, which enables them to meet the conditions for performing the occlusion procedure. These cases suggest that PADN is a novel therapeutic approach for patients with congenital heart disease who are in the gray zone of pulmonary arterial hypertension.

caption.p: Timeline of Presentation for Cases 1 and 2

ASD = atrial septal defect; Echo = echocardiography; PASP = pulmonary artery systolic pressure; PADN = pulmonary artery denervation ablation; PASP = pulmonary artery systolic pressure; RHC = right heart catheterization.

sec.title: History of presentation
sec.p:
A 33-year-old woman who had been experiencing chest tightness and shortness of breath for the past year was admitted to our hospital. Echocardiography on November 30, 2023, revealed a secundum atrial septal defect (ASD) (size: 28 mm) with a bidirectional shunt, predominantly left-to-right. Secondary complications included enlargement of the right atrium and ventricle, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) with a pulmonary artery systolic pressure (PASP) of 98 mm Hg, and a minimal amount of pericardial effusion. Physical examination revealed a heart rate of 70 beats/min with a regular rhythm, a grade 3 systolic murmur audible between the second and third intercostal spaces at the left sternal border, and an accentuated P2.Take-Home Message
•
Sequential therapy using targeted drugs, PADN, and transcatheter closure for atrial septal defect–associated pulmonary arterial hypertension is an investigational therapy that may benefit congenital heart disease patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension by reducing pulmonary vascular resistance and improving candidacy for advanced interventions in the future.

sec.title: Past medical history
sec.p: The patient had a history of hypertension and diabetes mellitus, with no history of lung disease or autoimmune diseases. There was no family history of genetic diseases, and she did not have a history of smoking, alcohol consumption, or use of weight-loss drugs.

sec.title: Investigations
sec.p: Electrocardiogram revealed sinus rhythm QRS electrical axis of the right reverse clock to the transposition T-wave change (T wave in III aVF V2 to V5 lead duplex, shallow inversion). Echocardiography findings indicated severe congenital heart disease, secondary porous ASD, bidirectional shunt with left-to-right dominance, secondary right atrial enlargement with moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and severe PAH.

sec.title: Management
sec.p: The patient was admitted on November 30, 2023, for right heart catheterization. Her systolic, diastolic, and mean pulmonary artery pressures were 85, 24, and 48 mm Hg, respectively. The pulmonary-to-systemic flow ratio (Qp/Qs) was 1.8, and the pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) was 5.4 Wood units (WU). After transcatheter occlusion, the systolic, diastolic, and mean pulmonary artery pressures were 83, 20, and 45 mm Hg, respectively, with no significant decrease. As such, the occluder was removed. The patient was then administered a combination of macitentan and sildenafil for 6 months; subsequent echocardiography showed a PASP of 80 mm Hg. Consequently, the treatment was adjusted to a triple-therapy regimen comprising selexipag, macitentan, and riociguat.

On January 15, 2025, the patient underwent percutaneous pulmonary artery denervation ablation (PADN) (Pulnovo Medical), and she continued triple-targeted PAH drug therapy postprocedure (Table 1, Figure 1).Table 1
Comparison of Hemodynamic Variables Before and After PADN in Case 1
PreoperativePostoperative
Arterial pressure, systolic/diastolic/mean
Right atrium14/–1/5 mm Hg13/–2/4 mm Hg
Right ventricle68/3/30 mm Hg69/–4/25 mm Hg
Pulmonary artery70/26/46 mm Hg62/22/40 mm Hg
Pulmonary artery wedge pressure6/–4/1 mm Hg5/–4/2 mm Hg
Cardiac output11.9 L/min12.0 L/min
PVR3.75 WU3.25 WU
Qp/Qs2.212.65
PADN = pulmonary artery denervation ablation; PVR = pulmonary vascular resistance; Qp/Qs = pulmonary-to-systemic flow ratio.Figure 1
Pulmonary Artery Denervation Ablation in Case 1
(A) Pulmonary arteriography. (B) Pulmonary artery denervation ablation.

Two months later, on March 17, 2025, the patient was admitted for percutaneous ASD closure. Upon right heart catheterization, the systolic, diastolic, and mean pulmonary artery pressures were 62, 23, and 40 mm Hg, respectively. The Qp/Qs was 2.5, and the PVR was 3.41 WU. As the atrial defect diameter was 25 mm, a 32-mm ASD occluder was selected. The postoperative systolic, diastolic, and mean pulmonary artery pressures were 51, 20, and 32 mm Hg, respectively. Echocardiography revealed appropriate position, size, and shape of the occluder, which had no effect on the surrounding structure (Figure 2).Figure 2
Echocardiography Before and After ASD Closure in Case 1
(A) Preoperative echocardiography revealing an ASD. (B) Postocclusion echocardiography image. (C) Intraoperative occlusion procedure. (D) Image after device release. ASD = atrial septal defect.

sec.title: Outcome and follow-up
sec.p: Follow-up echocardiography performed at 3 months postoperatively revealed that the PASP had decreased to 43 mm Hg, and both the right atrium and ventricle had returned to their normal size. Targeted medications (selexipag, macitentan, and riociguat) were also continued to reduce PAH. Echocardiography at the 6-month follow-up showed the PASP was further reduced to 41 mm Hg. The triple-targeted drug therapy was changed to double therapy (macitentan and riociguat), and the patient's activity endurance was further improved.

sec.title: History of presentation
sec.p: A 29-year-old woman who had been experiencing chest tightness and shortness of breath for the past year was admitted to our hospital. Echocardiography conducted on June 14, 2024, revealed congenital heart disease, specifically a secundum ASD (size: 15 mm) with a bidirectional, predominantly left-to-right shunt. Secondary complications included enlargement of the right atrium and ventricle, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and severe PAH with a PASP of 66 mm Hg. Physical examination revealed a heart rate of 86 beats/min with a regular rhythm, grade 3 systolic murmur audible between the second and third intercostal spaces at the left sternal border, and accentuated P2.

sec.title: Management
sec.p: One month later, on July 16, 2024, the patient was admitted for right heart catheterization. The systolic, diastolic, and mean pulmonary artery pressures were 78, 30, and 46 mm Hg, respectively. The Qp/Qs was 1.6, and the PVR was 7.59 WU. The patient was treated with a combination of treprostinil, macitentan, and riociguat for 1 month. Subsequent echocardiography showed a PASP of 65 mm Hg.

On August 9, 2024, the patient underwent percutaneous PADN, and she continued triple-targeted PAH drug therapy after the procedure (Table 2).Table 2
Comparison of Hemodynamic Variables Before and After PADN in Case 2
PreoperativePostoperative
Arterial pressure, systolic/diastolic/mean
Right atrium13/4/7 mm Hg10/2/6 mm Hg
Right ventricle65/–2/9 mm Hg59/–1/3 mm Hg
Pulmonary artery63/24/40 mm Hg55/22/35 mm Hg
Pulmonary artery wedge pressure14/8/11 mm Hg15/7/11 mm Hg
Cardiac output4.73 L/min6.13 L/min
PVR6.13 WU3.91 WU
Qp/Qs2.12.6
PADN = pulmonary artery denervation ablation; PVR = pulmonary vascular resistance; Qp/Qs = pulmonary-to-systemic flow ratio.

On February 4, 2025, the patient was admitted for percutaneous ASD closure. Upon right heart catheterization, the systolic, diastolic, and mean pulmonary artery pressures were 45, 13, and 25 mm Hg, respectively; the Qp/Qs ratio was 2.5, and the PVR was 3.5 WU. The atrial defect diameter was 15 × 11 mm, leading to the selection of a 26-mm ASD occluder. Postoperatively, the systolic, diastolic, and mean pulmonary artery pressures were 40, 10, and 22 mm Hg, respectively. Subsequent echocardiography confirmed appropriate position, size, and shape of the occluder, with no adverse effects on the surrounding structures (Figure 3).Figure 3
Echocardiography Before and After ASD Closure in Case 2
(A) Echocardiography indicating ASD. (B) Postocclusion echocardiography image. ASD = atrial septal defect.

sec.title: Outcome and follow-up
sec.p: The patient's follow-up echocardiography at 3 months postocclusion indicated that the PASP was approximately 45 mm Hg, and the size of the right heart had significantly improved compared with the preocclusion size. Hence, medications aimed at reducing the PAH (treprostinil, macitentan, and riociguat) were continued. At 6 months postocclusion, the PASP further decreased to 32 mm Hg, demonstrating a marked reduction in PAH. The triple-targeted drug therapy was changed to double therapy (macitentan and riociguat).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this report, we describe 2 cases of sequential therapy using targeted drugs, PADN, and transcatheter closure in patients with severe PAH associated with ASD. Both patients underwent triple-targeted drug therapy followed by PADN to achieve eligibility for transcatheter closure.

In the first patient, after 1 year of triple-targeted therapy, her PVR was decreased from 5.4 to 3.75 WU. However, her mean pulmonary artery pressure remained elevated at 46 mm Hg. Consequently, we performed PADN to improve the hemodynamic parameters, reducing the mean pulmonary artery pressure to 40 mm Hg and the PVR to 3.25 WU. Two months after the procedure, the hemodynamic variables were similar, and transcatheter closure was performed. In the second patient, after completing a 1-month protocol of triple-targeted therapy, the PVR decreased from 7.59 to 6.13 WU. Nevertheless, her mean pulmonary artery pressure continued to remain high at 40 mm Hg. PADN was performed to reduce the mean pulmonary artery pressure to 35 mm Hg and the PVR to 3.9 WU. Six months later, right heart catheterization found the hemodynamic variables were stable, and transcatheter closure was finally performed.

These 2 cases suggest that PADN can further significantly improve hemodynamic variables (ie, reduce the pulmonary artery pressure and PVR) in patients who receive adequate targeted drug therapy, eventually enabling them to meet the conditions for undergoing the occlusion procedure. This marks to our knowledge the first instance where “sequential therapy” is proposed for patients with severe PAH associated with ASD in the diagnostic “gray zone.”

Therapeutic strategies for the treatment of ASD with severe PAH are controversial. Treatments for PAH have dramatically advanced owing to the development of PAH-specific medications, including phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors, endothelin receptor antagonists, soluble guanylate cyclase inhibitors, and prostacyclines.1 These medical treatments improve survival in patients with PAH.2 Recently, a few studies have reported that PAH-specific medications and subsequent transcatheter closure (ie, the treat-and-repair strategy) are effective for ASD with severe PAH.3, 4, 5, 6 Nevertheless, a significant number of patients with PAH associated with ASD remain in a diagnostic gray zone, failing to adequately satisfy the hemodynamic criteria in current guidelines7 despite sufficient pharmacological therapy. In such cases, PADN can offer a complementary therapeutic method and option.

PADN is a novel technique for treating PAH. The underlying mechanism is that a high-frequency radiofrequency current is administered via a catheter to thermally ablate the lesion site, inducing the evaporation of intracellular and extracellular water, which results in cellular desiccation, shrinkage, and exfoliation, culminating in aseptic necrosis. Consequently, the damage of sympathetic nerves affects neurotransmitter release and the activation of adrenergic receptors in the pulmonary arteries. In a first-in-human study, PADN reduced pulmonary arterial pressures and improved exercise capacity at the 3-month follow-up.8 A subsequent study demonstrated improved hemodynamics, exercise performance, and cardiac function 1 year after PADN.9 In a multicenter randomized controlled study, PADN improved exercise capacity, hemodynamics, and clinical outcomes during 6 months of follow-up in patients with World Health Organization Group I PAH.10 However, these studies did not enroll patients with congenital heart disease–associated PAH. It may be because PADN alone, without occlusion, cannot completely eradicate congenital heart disease–associated PAH. As such, whether PADN has a therapeutic effect in these patients remains unclear, and no relevant studies or clinical case reports have been published to date.

The 2 cases in this study demonstrated, to our knowledge for the first time, that this technique had good short- to medium-term effects on such patients. However, there exist some limitations. Firstly, the number of cases in this study was relatively small and the follow-up time was short, requiring a larger sample size and more follow-up to verify this issue. Secondly, no control group was included in this study. Future controlled trials are warranted to validate these findings.

sec.title: Funding Support and Author Disclosures
sec.p: Support was received from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82270377) and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2703902). The authors have reported that they have no relationships relevant to the contents of this paper to disclose.
